questions.question: Explain the concept of the vtable and vptr in C++ and how they support runtime polymorphism. Provide a brief example.
keyPoints: vtable is a static table of function pointers per class
each object contains a vptr pointing to its class's vtable
virtual function calls resolved at runtime via vptr/vtable
example: base class pointer calling derived class method

questions.question: What is the slicing problem in C++? How can it be avoided using pointers or references, and what role does the 'override' keyword play in preventing accidental mis‑matches?
keyPoints: Slicing occurs when a derived object is assigned to a base object, losing derived parts
Use base class pointers or references to preserve the derived object
Declare virtual functions to enable polymorphic behavior
'override' ensures the function signature matches a virtual function in the base

questions.question: Design a class hierarchy for a graphical drawing application: an abstract base class Shape with pure virtual functions draw() and move(). Implement derived classes Circle and Rectangle. Discuss how virtual destructors ensure proper cleanup when objects are deleted through base class pointers, demonstrate safe down‑casting using dynamic_cast, and show the use of the 'override' specifier to avoid subtle bugs.
keyPoints: Shape as abstract class with pure virtual functions = runtime polymorphism
Virtual destructor in base ensures derived destructors are called
dynamic_cast<Derived*>(basePtr) checks actual type at runtime
'override' after function declarations prevents accidental overload

questions.question: Explain the two major forms of polymorphism in C++ (compile‑time and runtime). Describe how runtime polymorphism is achieved through virtual functions, pure virtual functions, and abstract classes. Provide a real‑world scenario—e.g., a payment processing system with CreditCard, PayPal, and UPI—where runtime polymorphism is essential. Discuss the role of vtable/vptr, how slicing can be avoided, and illustrate the use of dynamic_cast and the 'override' keyword.
keyPoints: Compile‑time (templates, overloading) vs runtime (virtual functions) polymorphism
Pure virtual functions create abstract base classes enforcing interface
Real‑world example: payment processing via base class pointer to derived objects
dynamic_cast for safe down‑cast, override to guarantee virtual function override
